text: Huka Falls to Aratiatia Rapids Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 45 min. Several times a day the gates at the Aratiatia dam are opened to release the full flow of the river - a spectacular sight from the lookout points. Stay safe and keep clear of the rapids at all times.

title: Peregrine Loop Track
text: Peregrine Loop Track. Difficulty: Advanced, Expert. The Peregrine Loop Track is a walking and mountain biking track with excellent views of Kawarau Gorge and goldmining heritage sites.

title: Flat Top Hill Tramping Track
text:
Flat Top Hill Tramping Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 hr. This loop track is an extension of the Basin View Tramping Track. It has a 300 m climb 
with extensive views.

title: Cairnmuir Hill Track
text: Cairnmuir Hill Track. Difficulty: Advanced. An all-day walk from Clyde to Bannockburn over the crest of the Cairnmuir Mountains.

title: Hikuwai Link Track
text: Hikuwai Link Track. Difficulty: Easy. A popular, gently-graded link track that connects Gunn Road with the Outlet Track.

title: Outlet Track
text: Outlet Track. Difficulty: Easy. A picturesque riverside walk along the Clutha River/Mata-Au. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Prospect Hill Track
text: Prospect Hill Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 3–4 hrs. From this track on a clear day you can see the Manuherikia basin, the Old Woman, Dunstan and Raggedy ranges.

title: Omeo Gully Track
text: Omeo Gully Track. Difficulty: Expert. Omeo Gully Track goes through one of Otago’s highest, most isolated gold fields. View distinctive gold workings on high sluice faces, deeply riveted tail races, tall stacked tailings and the remains of dwellings.

title: Kopuwai Track to Sisters Track
text: Kopuwai Track to Sisters Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 1 hr 30 min one way. Centred in the heart of the Kopuwai Conservation Area, this track follows along the rolling tops of the Old Man Range.

title: Obelisk Loop Track
text: Obelisk Loop Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 to 3 hr. This loop starts from the highest point on the range at Kopuwai. On a good day, you can see as far out as Rakiura/Stewart Island, Aoraki/Mt Cook, and Mt Aspiring/Tititea.

title: The Sisters Track
text: The Sisters Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Tramp through herb fields and snow tussock while enjoying great views of the Alexandra basin, Old Woman Range and The Remarkables Mountain range.

title: Hāwea River Track
text: Hāwea River Track. Difficulty: Easy. Suitable for all ages and experience levels, this gentle riverside track links Albert Town to Hāwea. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Potters No. 2 Tramping Track, Huts to Grave
text: Potters No. 2 Tramping Track, Huts to Grave. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 1 hr return. This is a short tramping track through the protected historic site in the impressive landscape of the Campbell Creek catchment.

title: Luggate Creek Track
text: Luggate Creek Track. Difficulty: Easy. This short, easy amble leads from Luggate township down to the Clutha River.

title: Nicholsons Track
text: Nicholsons Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 1 hr return. This short track takes you from the top of Symes Road through beautiful tussock country to Nicholson Hut.

title: Rockvale Track
text: Rockvale Track. Difficulty: Advanced. The northernmost access track to the Rock and Pillar Conservation Area.

title: Stonehurst Track
text: Stonehurst Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Go up and over the Rock and Pillar Range.

title: Newcastle Track
text: Newcastle Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Suitable for biking and walking this track follows the true left of the Clutha River from Hāwea River swing bridge to Kane Road.

title: Little Criffel Track
text: Little Criffel Track. Difficulty: Advanced. This track is in the Pisa Conservation Area which lies between Wānaka and Cromwell.

title: Pukaki Flats walk
text: Pukaki Flats walk. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr. The Pukaki Flats walk goes over an open block of hard tussock grasslands just north of Twizel. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Greta Track
text: Greta Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 4 to 5 hrs. Pass by fellfields (blocky scree) and short rock bluffs above Gretas Stream. This track is close to Lake Ōhau.

title: Maunga Kākaramea Loop
text: Maunga Kākaramea Loop. Difficulty: Easy, Advanced. Estimated biking time: 3.5 hours. Ride through a geothermal field with this series of tracks that connect to form a mountain biking loop.

title: Cowans Hill Track
text: Cowans Hill Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr. The track passes through exotic forest, open tussock country and past small tarns where wetland birds may stop to feed. It is suitable for walkers and mountain bikers.

title: Tenahaun Conservation Area tramping tracks
text: Tenahaun Conservation Area tramping tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. The Tenahaun Conservation Area includes walks in the Moorhouse Range and Rangitata Gorge. The Moorhouse Range can be mountain biked along the easement only.

title: Tarnbrae Track A2O
text: Tarnbrae Track A2O. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 3 hr one way. Traverse glacial terraces through Freehold Creek beech forest. Part of the Alps 2 Ocean (A2O) Cycle Trail, the high point on this track and offers fabulous views of Lake Ohau and the surrounding country.

title: Aorangi Crossing
text: Aorangi Crossing. Cross the Aorangi Range from Putangirua Pinnacles to Cape Palliser.

title: Paparoa Track: Pororari Hut Track
text: Paparoa Track: Pororari Hut Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 hr 30 min - 3 hr 30 min one way. Walk to Pororari Hut for 360 degree views and experience a shorter (overnight) version of the Paparoa Track Great Walk.

metadata.biking_time: 2 hr 30 min - 3 hr 30 min one way

title: Rock Peak Track
text: Rock Peak Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Get great views from this track at the southern end of the Pisa range.

title: Mount Robertson tracks
text: Mount Robertson tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Mountain bike or walk through mature and regenerating forest, with the option to visit the Mt Robertson/Tokomaru summit.

title: Waitahu - Murray Connecting Track
text: Waitahu - Murray Connecting Track. Difficulty: Expert. This historic pack track links Murray Creek with Waitahu valley. It's part of a popular mountain biking circuit.

title: Paparoa Track
text: Paparoa Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 days one way. The Paparoa Track crosses the Paparoa Range. It takes you through alpine tops, limestone karst landscapes, thriving rainforests and provides breath-taking views.

title: Arawata Bridle Track
text: Arawata Bridle Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. The Arawata Bridle Track follows part of the original bridle trail that connected Queenstown and Glenorchy.

title: Awa Nohoaka Conservation Area access tracks
text: Awa Nohoaka Conservation Area access tracks. Difficulty: Advanced. Short tussock and pasture grasslands contrast with grey shrublands, dry shrublands and kanuka shrublands make up this unique conservation area.

title: Carrick Range access tracks
text: Carrick Range access tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Rising above Bannockburn and rich in goldmining history, the Carrick Range is one of the most accessible mountain ranges in Central Otago. These tracks take you to three conservation areas and two historic reserves.

title: Mt Difficulty tracks
text: Mt Difficulty tracks. Difficulty: Advanced. Enjoy fantastic views of Gibbston Valley and Cromwell basin along the way, and out to the Doolan mountains from the top.

title: Slapjack Saddle Track
text: Slapjack Saddle Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Journey through the Carrick Range, past the spectacular Long Gully bluffs. There are brilliant views from the tops and golden colours in the lower reaches during autumn.

title: Moerangi Track
text: Moerangi Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 5 - 7 hr. This shared-use track includes an overnight stop at an historic hut and amazing views from the top of the Moerangi saddle (955 m). Listen for the piercing calls of kiwi at night.

title: The Old Ghost Road
text: The Old Ghost Road. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 - 3 days. This old gold miners' road is being revived as a tramping and mountain biking track. It traverses native forests, tussock tops, river flats and valleys with stunning views of the magnificent Mōkihinui Gorge and surrounds.

title: Hunter Valley tracks
text: Hunter Valley tracks. Difficulty: Advanced. The Hunter River Valley is part of the Hāwea Conservation Park and nestled between the Young and Huxley ranges. Its open grasslands, braided river system and deep canyon gorges offer remote seekers a beautiful but challenging backcountry experience.

title: Waihāhā Hut Track
text: Waihāhā Hut Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2-3 hr one way. Walk to Waihāhā Hut for a short overnight trip.

title: Tirohanga Dunes Trail
text: Tirohanga Dunes Trail. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 2-3 hr return. On this easy, beautiful trail, you’ll enjoy panoramic views of the Pacific Ocean, Whakaari/White Island, Moutohora/Whale Island and towards East Cape.

title: Waikakaho/Cullen Creek Track
text: Waikakaho/Cullen Creek Track. Difficulty: Advanced. This walkway follows an old goldminer's trail across the range between the Wairau Valley and Linkwater. It can be walked or ridden in one day, but you will need to arrange transport between the road ends.

title: Okiwi Bay Seaward Valley Track
text: Okiwi Bay Seaward Valley Track. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Estimated biking time: 4 - 5 hr. Walk or mountain bike through varied vegetation and enjoy ocean views.

title: Minaret Burn Track
text: Minaret Burn Track. Difficulty: Expert. This walk passes through native forest and bush, crossing burns (streams) and giving access to Lake Wānaka itself. This track is suitable for walking, mountain biking and horse riding.

title: Lake Roxburgh Walkway
text: Lake Roxburgh Walkway. Difficulty: Intermediate. This historic trail follows an old bridle path that transported coal to the gold mining dredges 1890s-1930s.

title: Dingle Burn Peninsula Track
text: Dingle Burn Peninsula Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. The Dingle Burn Peninsula Track is a pleasant walk to the Turihuka Conservation Area. It also provides access into Hāwea Conservation Park via the Dingle Burn and Hunter River Tracks.

title: Patutu Route
text: Patutu Route. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Climb to the summit of Patutu (1162 m) for great views of the coast and the North Island.

title: Baikie Hut Track
text: Baikie Hut Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr 30 min one way. Baikie Hut makes a great picnic or overnight venue.

title: Yeoman's Track
text: Yeoman's Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2 - 3 hr. This forest track follows an old route where logs were once hauled to Yeoman’s Mill. It is open to walkers and mountain bikers.

title: Whirinaki Forest Mountain Bike Track
text: Whirinaki Forest Mountain Bike Track. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2 - 4 hr. The Whirinaki Forest Mountain Bike Track is purpose built and weaves through one of the most spectacular and interesting forests in the world.

title: Wharfedale/Townshend Track
text: Wharfedale/Townshend Track. Difficulty: Easy, Advanced. Follow this track to Wharfedale Hut by walking, mountain biking, or horse riding.

title: Whareroa Farm tracks
text: Whareroa Farm tracks. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate. Enjoy regenerating shrubland, coastal views and kohekohe forest on these walks that are suitable for children.

title: Whakaipo Bay tracks
text: Whakaipo Bay tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2 hr | 3 hr 30 min. Walk or ride over the headland between Whakaipo and Whangamata bays and enjoy the lake views and native bush.

title: Western Ōkataina Walkway
text: Western Ōkataina Walkway. Difficulty: Intermediate. Western Ōkataina Walkway is open for both walking and mountain biking.

title: Wakamarina Track
text: Wakamarina Track. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced, Expert. Follow in the footsteps of old goldminers on the Wakamarina Track to cross the Richmond Range from the Wairau Valley to the Wakamarina Valley.

title: Wairepo Kettleholes Track
text: Wairepo Kettleholes Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 30 min one way. Wairepo Kettleholes provide a feeding area for both native and introduced birds.

title: Waihāhā and Waihora tracks
text: Waihāhā and Waihora tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 1 hr 30 min | 2 hr. Explore the remote bush, cliffs and waterfalls of the Western Bays of Lake Taupō. Normally accessible only by boat, these trails take you through a variety of landscapes with fantastic lookout points.

title: Upper Clutha River Track
text: Upper Clutha River Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Walk or cycle along the banks of the Clutha River near Lake Wānaka.

title: Twizel River Trail
text: Twizel River Trail. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 2 hr one way. This is a lovely walk alongside Twizel River.

title: Twelve Mile Delta to Bob’s Cove Track
text: Twelve Mile Delta to Bob’s Cove Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 90 min return. Follow the shore of Lake Wakatipu and enjoy lake views, native bush and ancient marine sediments. Twelve Mile Delta was a location for Lord of the Rings' Ithilien Camp.

title: Tuhoto Ariki Trail
text: Tuhoto Ariki Trail. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 40 min. Specifically designed dual use trail through native bush with great downhills and some decent climbs for the mountain bikers. Starts from the highest point in Whakarewarewa Forest.

title: Tree Trunk Gorge tracks
text: Tree Trunk Gorge tracks. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: Various times under 4 hr. Walk or mountain bike through the Kaimanawa Forest Park taking in two spectacular gorges, and the characteristic beech and kamahi forest.

title: Tongariro River Trail
text: Tongariro River Trail. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 30 min - 1 hr 30 min. Follow the banks of the Tongariro River alongside farmland, through native bush and urban areas, enjoying magnificent river views. An easy, mainly flat track with multiple entry points - a great family friendly option.

title: The Timber Trail: Pureora - Ongarue
text: The Timber Trail: Pureora - Ongarue. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate. Estimated biking time: Full trail: 2-3 days. Using historic bush tramways, old bulldozer and haul roads, and newly constructed track, this Grade 2/3 trail features 35 bridges, including some of the highest and longest in New Zealand. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Te Waiti Hut Walk
text: Te Waiti Hut Walk. Difficulty: Intermediate. This track affords views of Te Waiti Stream and lowland forest and is suitable for a family day trip.

title: Te Iringa Track
text: Te Iringa Track. Difficulty: Expert. Estimated biking time: 4 - 6 hr one way. On this challenging and remote adventure ride, pass through beech forest and enjoy the scenery, streams and wildlife of the Kaimanawa Forest Park.

title: Te Ara Pātaka/Summit Walkway
text: Te Ara Pātaka/Summit Walkway. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Estimated biking time: Various options. Enjoy magnificent views over Banks Peninsula, the Canterbury plains, Southern Alps and Pacific Ocean on this track, which is about an hour's drive from Christchurch.

title: Te Ara a Waiau Walkway/Cycleway
text: Te Ara a Waiau Walkway/Cycleway. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr. Walk or cycle through bush from Franz Josef township to the Franz Josef Glacier car park.

title: Tasman River tracks
text: Tasman River tracks. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 2 hr. Tasman River is a large braided river which flows into Lake Pukaki. There are walking and cycling tracks in the area.

title: Studholme Bush Scenic Reserve tracks
text: Studholme Bush Scenic Reserve tracks. Difficulty: Easiest, Intermediate. Enjoy a wide range of tracks, from an easy, fun cycle with the family along Railway Track, to a steep 50-minute walk to the Whitehorse monument with great views out over Waimate.

title: Stanley River Route
text: Stanley River Route. Difficulty: Expert. This marked route links Charlies Saddle to Stanley Vale Hut in the St James Conservation Area.

title: St James Cycle Trail
text: St James Cycle Trail. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Estimated biking time: 6 - 9 hr/1 - 2 days. This trail offers challenging, mixed riding through stunning scenery of mountain peaks, crystal clear waters, high country lakes, alpine meadows, subalpine beech forest, and expansive grassy flats.

title: Seven Mile Point Track
text: Seven Mile Point Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Walk or bike by Lake Wakatipu, then climb through native bush and pine forest to see Wilson Bay from Kirks Terrace. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Rotary Ride and Waikato River Track
text: Rotary Ride and Waikato River Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 30 min | 20 min. These two tracks along the banks of the Waikato River allow for stunning views of the river, and access to Huka Falls and the network of mountain bike trails in the area.

title: Remutaka Rail Trail
text: Remutaka Rail Trail. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr 30 min bike from Kaitoke. Built in 1878, this rail route between the Hutt Valley and Featherston is now a gently graded 18 km walk, run or mountain bike ride, with opportunities for camping, swimming and fishing.

title: Richmond Trail
text: Richmond Trail. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 - 3 hr. Enjoy stunning views of Lake Tekapo while walking or riding this track. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Queen Charlotte Track
text: Queen Charlotte Track. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced, Expert. Experience the spectacular Marlborough Sounds by walking or biking the Queen Charlotte Track. The track stretches 73.5 km  from historic Meretoto/Ship Cove through to Anakiwa in Queen Charlotte Sound. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Point Elizabeth Walkway
text: Point Elizabeth Walkway. A stunning, well surfaced, coastal walkway. Enjoy walking through varied and interesting forest to a panoramic, cliff top viewing platform. Point Elizabeth track is a perfect walk for families and children.

title: Pavilion Peak Track
text: Pavilion Peak Track. Difficulty: Expert. Estimated biking time: 6 - 8 hr. This track traverses a range in the Lindis Conservation Area.

title: Pakihi Track
text: Pakihi Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2 hr 30 min - 3 hr 30 min. The Pakihi Track is a stunning but challenging 20 km journey through lush forest for trampers and mountain bikers.

title: Otago Central Rail Trail
text: Otago Central Rail Trail. Difficulty: Easy. Pedalling Otago’s rural heart. The famous Rail Trail takes you through traditional country towns and sheep-farming land farmed by families for many generations.

title: Opouri Bridle Track
text: Opouri Bridle Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Walk or mountain bike through native forest and farmland from Opouri Saddle down to Harvey Bay (Duncan Bay).

title: Okahukura Loop
text: Okahukura Loop. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 3 hr. Okahukura Loop is for more skilled riders, but is not particularly technical.

title: Ohakune Old Coach Road
text: Ohakune Old Coach Road. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2 hr 20 min. Take a trip back into New Zealand's rail heritage while you walk or cycle an old coach road. The road was used to carry passengers and goods between two railheads on the North Island main trunk link before they were linked in 1908.

title: Nydia Track
text: Nydia Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 5 hr - 8 hr. The 27 km Nydia Track offers walkers and cyclists great views and varied track conditions. Plan on an overnight stop at Nydia Bay where you can explore the history and coastline.

title: Nevis Valley tracks
text: Nevis Valley tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. Access the Remarkables Conservation Area on these tracks.

title: Mt Patriarch Route
text: Mt Patriarch Route. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 3 hr return. Walk or mountain bike up Mt Patriarch (1,656 m).

title: Mt Judah / Heather Jock Tracks
text: Mt Judah / Heather Jock Tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. Scheelite mining history, open tussock country and grand views await on these tracks - with the option of staying overnight in the basic Heather Jock Hut.

title: Moonlight Pack Track
text: Moonlight Pack Track. Difficulty: Advanced. The Moonlight Pack Track offers the opportunity to see reminders of the gold mining history of the area, and to walk through West Coast native beech forest.

title: Moke Lake Loop Track
text: Moke Lake Loop Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. This popular walk takes you on a loop around the lake. Superb views are on offer as you travel through grassland surrounded by mountains.

title: Mavora - Greenstone Walkway
text: Mavora - Greenstone Walkway. This is a 50 km, four day tramping trip, linking the Mavora Lakes Camping Area with the Greenstone Track. It passes through open valley tussock land and beech forested hill country. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Mangapurua/Kaiwhakauka Track
text: Mangapurua/Kaiwhakauka Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 1 day. The Mangapurua and Kaiwhakauka Tracks are two stages of the Mountains to Sea Cycle Trail. There are various entry/exit options. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Mananui Tramline (Māhinapua Walkway)
text: Mananui Tramline (Māhinapua Walkway). Difficulty: Easy. This flat, easy walk or mountain bike ride, suitable for children, follows an old logging tramline and passes beautiful Lake Māhinapua.

title: Macaulay Hut Track
text: Macaulay Hut Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 3 hr one way. Macaulay valley can be accessed on foot, or by mountain bike, horse or 4WD.

title: Lindis Valley tracks
text: Lindis Valley tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. A range of tracks for walkers and mountain bikers alike, in an attractive, upland valley. Easily accessed off SH8 between Tarras and Omarama.

title: Lake Ōhau Track
text: Lake Ōhau Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr. The track runs along the Lake Ōhau shoreline at its moraine end, with great views of Ben Ohau Peak and Lake Ōhau. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Lake Kaniere Walkway
text: Lake Kaniere Walkway. Difficulty: Advanced, Expert. Walk or mountain bike along the western shores of Lake Kaniere through lush lakeside forest, passing many picturesque bays and numerous forest streams.

title: Lake Hayes Walkway
text: Lake Hayes Walkway. Difficulty: Intermediate. Stroll, walk, run or cycle around a lake surrounded by spectacular mountains. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Lake Hāwea Track
text: Lake Hāwea Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 6 - 8 hr. This provides access to one of the Hāwea Conservation Park's most beautiful areas, the Hunter Valley.

title: Fossickers Lake Track
text: Fossickers Lake Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 1 hr. The track loops around the Globe Progress Mine's former tailings storage facility, known as Fossickers Lake.

title: Lake Dispute Walkway
text: Lake Dispute Walkway. Difficulty: Intermediate, Advanced. Zigzag up through kōhūhū/pittosporum shrubland to Lake Dispute high country lake.

title: Lake Dispute to Moke Lake Track
text: Lake Dispute to Moke Lake Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Follow a 4WD track beside Lake Dispute and into high county farmland to reach the southern end of Moke Lake.

title: Lake Clearwater tracks
text: Lake Clearwater tracks. Difficulty: Easy. Enjoy views of Lake Clearwater from the lake shore or the nearby Mount Guy (1319 m).

title: Otamatakou Track
text: Otamatakou Track. Difficulty: Easy. Follow an old farm track to the reserve which contains remnant native shrubs.

title: Knuckle Hill Track
text: Knuckle Hill Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: Under 1 hr. From the top of Knuckle Hill are excellent views of the top of the South Island, particularly Farewell Spit and Whanganui Inlet.

title: Kiko Loop Track
text: Kiko Loop Track. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 45 min. This short loop track is a great way for walkers and mountain bikers to experience the variety of nature in the Kaimanawa Forest Park.

title: Kawakawa Bay tracks
text: Kawakawa Bay tracks. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 1 hr | 1 hr 30 min. The K2K and Orakau tracks both take you into the picturesque Kawakawa Bay; walk or ride the trails separately, or link them together for a longer day out.

title: Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway
text: Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway. Difficulty: Easiest. Situated at the base of the Coromandel Range, the Karangahake Gorge Historic Walkway follows the old railway line between Paeroa and Waihi, giving access to impressive remains from the mining and railway eras.

title: Nikau Walk
text: Nikau Walk. Difficulty: Easiest. Follow a stream through beautiful native forest with swimming and picnicking opportunities. This track is great for children. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Kaniere Water Race Walkway
text:
Kaniere Water Race Walkway. Difficulty: Easy, Advanced. Estimated biking time: 4 hr. The Kaniere Water Race Walkway provides an opportunity for
a pleasant bush walk in an area that is rich in history.

title: Kakepuku Track
text: Kakepuku Track. The Kakepuku Track in Kakepuku Historic Reserve climbs up through bush to the summit of Kakepuku, an old volcano.

title: Jack's Point Track
text: Jack's Point Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. A walk with superb lake and mountain views over undulating open grassland and scattered scrub, ending at Jack's Point.

title: Isthmus Peak Track
text: Isthmus Peak Track. Difficulty: Expert. Isthmus Peak Track is a steep and challenging but rewarding hike located between Lake Wānaka and Lake Hāwea, offering panoramic views of both lakes and the Southern Alps.

title: Hopkins valley tracks
text: Hopkins valley tracks. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 1 hr 30 min one way. Hopkins valley, in Ruataniwha Conservation Park, is near Lake Ōhau.

title: Homestead Run
text: Homestead Run. Difficulty: Intermediate. For young families or riders with insufficient time or experience to attempt the full St James Cycle Trail, the Homestead Run will give you a taste of St James.

title: Historic Kill Devil Pack Track
text: Historic Kill Devil Pack Track. Difficulty: Advanced, Expert. Estimated biking time: 1-2 days. This challenging mountain bike track provides spectacular views from the ridge tops of the Lockett Range and features historic huts and shelters.

title: Heaphy Track
text: Heaphy Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Estimated biking time: 2–3 days. Travel through expansive tussock downs, lush forests and nīkau palms to the roaring seas of the West Coast. Mountain bikes are allowed between 1 May and 30 November.

title: Hatupatu Trail
text: Hatupatu Trail. Difficulty: Expert. Hatupatu is an alternative ending to Tuhoto Ariki Trail leading back down the hill ending at the junction of Moerangi and Tawa roads. Near the exit of the mountain bike trail 'Billy T'.

title: Hakarimata Rail Trail
text: Hakarimata Rail Trail. Difficulty: Easiest. This family walking and cycling track follows the route of the former Ngāruawāhia/Glen Massey railway to attractive stream cascades.

title: Grandview Ridge tracks
text: Grandview Ridge tracks. Difficulty: Advanced. Highly visible over a large area of the surrounding landscape, the Grandview Ridge provides a spectacular vantage point for views over the upper Clutha Valley and peaks beyond.

title: Grandview Mountain Track
text: Grandview Mountain Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Trampers are well rewarded for their effort from the upper sections of the greater Grandview Range with inspiring views over Lakes Hāwea and Wānaka and mountains beyond.

title: Government Track
text: Government Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Walk or mountain bike the Government Track which traverses the slopes of the beech-and mānuka-forested Waipori Valley.

title: Godley River Route
text: Godley River Route. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 5 hr one way. Walk or mountain bike up the Godley valley to Red Stag Hut and Godley Hut.

title: Lake Emma to Lake Camp Track
text: Lake Emma to Lake Camp Track. Difficulty: Easy. Walk or mountain bike to an old musterers hut near Lake Emma.

title: Gibbs Hill Track
text: Gibbs Hill Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. The Gibbs Hill Track crosses Abel Tasman National Park inland between Wainui and Tōtaranui.

title: Fowlers Pass Track
text: Fowlers Pass Track. Difficulty: Expert. Fowlers Pass (1296 m) connects Fowlers Hut on Tophouse Road with Stanley Vale and Lake Guyon huts in the St James Conservation Area.

title: Forest Road (Port FitzRoy - Whangaparapara)
text: Forest Road (Port FitzRoy - Whangaparapara). Discover a wide array of beautiful bush and mountain views.

title: Flora Saddle to Barron Flat Track
text: Flora Saddle to Barron Flat Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 1 day. This backcountry ride traverses through a variety of landscapes and terrains following a historic pack track.

title: Ewe Range Track
text: Ewe Range Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. The Ewe Range Track provides walking access to the northern end of Oteake Conservation Park.

title: Dry Creek Track
text: Dry Creek Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Dry Creek Track provides great backcountry opportunities for short walks, tramping and mountain biking with access to the Dry Creek and Neinei i Kura Conservation areas.

title: Dromedary Hill Track
text: Dromedary Hill Track. Difficulty: Expert. Estimated biking time: 3 - 4 hr. It is well worth the effort getting out the car and stretching your legs with a day hike up Dromedary Hill.

title: Deans Bank Track
text: Deans Bank Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. This loop walking and mountain biking track meanders along the upper slopes of the Clutha river and offers spectacular views of surrounding peaks and Otago landscapes.

title: Croesus Track
text: Croesus Track. Difficulty: Advanced. Croesus Track is one of the finest surviving examples of a miners' track on the West Coast. Croesus Track is part of Paparoa Track and a premier mountain bike ride.

title: Coromandel Walkway and mountain bike track
text: Coromandel Walkway and mountain bike track. Difficulty: Expert. Walk or mountain bike between Fletcher Bay and Stony Bay.

title: Cloudy Peak Track
text: Cloudy Peak Track. Difficulty: Advanced. The Cloudy Peak Track from its 1526 m high point, provides fantastic views of the Upper Clutha plains and peaks of the Lindis and St Bathans ranges.

title: Christchurch to Little River Rail Trail
text: Christchurch to Little River Rail Trail. Difficulty: Easiest. The whole 46 km-long walkway and cycleway runs from Hornby in Christchurch to Little River on Banks Peninsula. The Motukārara to Little River section described here covers 20 km.

title: Butterfields Wetland Walk
text: Butterfields Wetland Walk. Difficulty: Easy. Situated near the Hāwea River Swing Bridge this small loop track around the Butterfields Wildlife Management Area is suitable for biking, running and walking.

title: Burtton's Track
text: Burtton's Track. Difficulty: Expert. Estimated biking time: 4 - 6 hours. Burtton's Track (Scotts Road to Mangahao Road) lies between Palmerston North and Shannon. This track is part of Te Araroa Trail.

title: Braemar Road to Landslip Creek
text: Braemar Road to Landslip Creek. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 2 hr. A public access easement leads to Landslip Creek above Lake Pukaki.

title: Bob’s Cove Track and Nature Walk
text: Bob’s Cove Track and Nature Walk. Difficulty: Easy. Estimated biking time: 10 min one way. This short walk passes through native bush to reach a stunning cove on Lake Wakatipu, with an optional climb to Picnic Point – it's a great option for kids.

title: Bannockburn Sluicings
text: Bannockburn Sluicings. Difficulty: Intermediate. This vanished landscape is New Zealand’s own wild west: The Bannockburn Sluicings are the scarred landscapes of a short-lived goldmining era.

title: Atleys Track
text: Atleys Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. You can see many historic gold mining features from Atleys Track and there are superb views from the summit of Mt Dewar.

title: Poulter River Track
text: Poulter River Track. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 2-4 hr. Poulter River Track is the only mountain biking track in Arthur’s Pass National Park. It has great scenery, some challenges and a good hut for an overnight stay.

title: Archer Track
text: Archer Track. Difficulty: Easy, Intermediate, Advanced. This walking and mountain biking track passes through native and pine forest from Penzance Bay to Elaine Bay.

title: Aratiatia Rapids Lookout Walk
text: Aratiatia Rapids Lookout Walk. Estimated biking time: 45 min. Several times a day the gates at the Aratiatia dam are opened to release the full flow of the river - a spectacular sight from the lookout points. Stay safe and keep clear of the rapids at all times.

title: Aorere Goldfields Track
text: Aorere Goldfields Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. Estimated biking time: 3-4 hours. Aorere Goldfields Track is a 3-hour loop, which gives you access to explore some of the more accessible gold-mining remnants.

title: Ahuriri Valley Track
text: Ahuriri Valley Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. This track follows the Ahuriri River. Three huts on route provide several options.

title: Glenroy Water Race Track
text: Glenroy Water Race Track. Difficulty: Intermediate. The track follows the historic Welshmen’s Water Race through several creeks, then steadily climbs to Ben Cruachan with good views of Lake Wakatipu, the Basin and Nevis Bluff.

# How to set up your mountain bike suspension for the best performance – sag, damping and other suspension tuning concepts explained

Our essential guide to MTB suspension setup, with simple walkthrough steps, will help your bike work at its best

[Finlay Anderson](https://www.bikeradar.com/author/finlay-anderson)

Published: August 10, 2025 at 2:00 pm

* * *

Getting your mountain bike suspension setup right can make all the difference. Most modern suspension works brilliantly if adjusted correctly, but finding the optimum settings can be tricky with so many adjustments and dials.

It’s worth persevering, though, because getting the setup of your [suspension fork](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/best-mountain-bike-suspension-forks) or [rear shock](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/best-mountain-bike-rear-shocks) right will enable your bike to work at its best, improving handling, grip and comfort. 

In this guide, we explain the fundamentals of setting up mountain bike suspension. We then examine what to do if your setup isn’t right for you, or if you want to tweak your setup further to suit your specific riding style and terrain.

This guide focuses on setting up air-sprung suspension because it’s far more common than coil-sprung suspension. However, we outline the key differences in setting up coil-sprung suspension at the end of the guide. 

### Navigation

  * [How to set sag](https://www.bikeradar.com/#sag)
  * [How to set damping](https://www.bikeradar.com/#damping)
  * [Suspension tuning](https://www.bikeradar.com/#suspension-tuning)
  * [The effects of different suspension adjustments](https://www.bikeradar.com/#effects-of-suspension-adjustments)
  * [Coil-sprung suspension tips](https://www.bikeradar.com/#coil-sprung-suspension)



## How to set up mountain bike suspension 

The video above shows how our former resident suspension guru, Seb Stott, set up his bikes. It should get your suspension performing well before you even leave the car park.

This video explains how to set your sag, test how progressive your suspension needs to be, and how to fine-tune your rebound damping.

Follow the steps below for more details. 

### How to set sag

Setting the sag should be the starting point of your suspension setup. Justin Sullivan / Our Media

When we talk about sag, we mean how much the suspension sinks into its [travel](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/how-much-suspension-travel-do-i-need-on-my-mountain-bike) when you sit on the bike. 

Not enough sag, and your bike will lack grip; too much, and it will wallow and won’t deal well with repeated hits.

Here's how to set the sag on your mountain bike in nine simple steps.

  1. Inflate the air spring in the fork or shock to the recommended pressure for your weight. This can be found in the manufacturer’s manual, on their website or, often in the case of forks, printed on the back of one leg. It’s a good starting point
  2. Check any external compression-damping dials or levers are set fully open
  3. Bounce on the bike, to ‘free up’ the suspension and help balance the air pressure in the negative spring
  4. Wearing your riding gear – helmet, shoes, pack, etc – get a friend to hold the bike so you can get into your normal seated (for shock sag) or standing (for fork sag) position. Hold this for about five seconds
  5. Ensure that the rubber O-ring around the fork stanchion/shock shaft is pushed up against the wiper seal
  6. Carefully dismount from the bike without disturbing the O-ring
  7. Measure the distance (in millimetres) that the O-ring has moved up the stanchion/shaft. Divide this by the fork’s travel (eg. 160mm) or the shock’s stroke length (eg. 50mm), then multiply that by 100 to find the sag percentage. Helpfully, this is already marked on [RockShox forks](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/rockshox-suspension-fork-guide) and shocks. Bikes with [hidden shocks](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/hidden-mountain-bike-shocks) often have a sag meter on the main suspension pivot
  8. Check the brand’s recommended sag settings, which can be found in the manual, but are usually between 15 and 20 per cent for forks, and 25 and 30 per cent for shocks
  9. Add air (for less sag) or remove it (for more) to achieve the desired sag percentage. You can adjust these figures to suit your riding, but not by more than a few per cent, because this will upset the bike’s dynamic geometry.

### How to set damping

Once the sag (and thereby, spring rate) has been set, it's time to dial the damping setup. Andy Lloyd / Our Media

Damping is a typically oil-based system that controls how fast the spring [compresses and rebounds](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/workshop/compression-and-rebound-adjustment). 

Many forks and shocks have external dials or levers to tweak this. 

These have to cover a wide range of spring rates (air pressures), so there’ll usually only be two or three ‘clicks’ of adjustment you can use to fine-tune things for your weight, riding style and the terrain. Generally, high-end forks and shocks will offer a greater range of adjustment.

Lighter riders will be at one end of the settings range (lower pressures), and heavier folk will be towards the other (higher pressures). 

To set your damping, make sure you've got the right sag, then head to a familiar trail that's typical of your riding. Then, follow the steps below.

### 1\. Set your rebound damping

Rebound plays a large role in how your bike grips and handles. Ian Linton / Our Media

Rebound damping controls how quickly your suspension resets after absorbing an impact and should be set up first. Too slow, and the suspension will feel sluggish and may not extend quickly enough after an impact to absorb the next one. But too fast, and the bike will bounce off obstacles, breaking grip. How fast you’ll want the rebound will depend on your weight, speed, style and terrain.

  1. Starting with the rear shock (if your bike has one), turn the dial/lever towards the ‘+’ or tortoise symbol, one click at a time, until the shock only oscillates once after a good-sized compression, such as riding off a kerb
  2. Session the trail section and decrease the damping – by turning the dial/lever towards the ‘-’ or jackalope (hare) symbol – one click at a time until the suspension can track the ground and recover between hits
  3. Match the fork’s rebound feel to the shock using the same method



Note: if you have RockShox’s latest Charger 3.1 damper, the meaning of the markings is reversed, so turning dials towards ‘+’ reduces damping and vice versa.

#### 2\. Set your spring curve

While they don't look fancy, volume spacers can make a big difference to your suspension. Andy Lloyd / Our Media

If you’ve got your sag and rebound set correctly but use too much travel too often, you’ll need to make the spring curve more progressive by installing [volume spacers](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/volume-spacers). 

These reduce the volume of the air spring, making it ramp up more firmly towards the end of its stroke. If your settings are correct but you can’t reach full travel, try removing any volume spacers, one at a time. Remember to record your pressures before releasing any air from the fork or shock, to ensure your sag stays consistent.

####  3\. Set your compression damping

Compression damping enables you to tune how readily the suspension uses its travel. Ian Linton / Our Media

Now that the spring supports your weight, you can adjust the compression damping. Some forks and shocks only have low-speed (LSC) adjustment, which controls how the suspension responds to pedalling, pumping and weight shifts under braking. Others also have high-speed (HSC) adjustment, which limits how fast it can move to absorb impacts.

  1. Start with the recommended settings (or LSC in the middle, HSC fully open).
  2. Session the trail section and turn the dial one or two clicks in each direction until you get the suspension behaving in the way that best suits the terrain and your riding style. Compression damping reduces suspension sensitivity, so only add as much as is needed to increase support.
  3. Once you’ve done this with the rear shock, do the same with the fork. 



#### 4\. Final checks

Now it's time to get out on your bike and feel the suspension changes for yourself. Scott Windsor / Our Media

Getting the balance right between the front and rear suspension is vital to get the best performance from a bike. 

If the fork is overly firm, supportive or fast compared to the rear end, this will shift the bike’s weight balance backwards, affecting handling negatively. 

The opposite is also true – and potentially more dangerous. If you bounce the front and rear suspension together in a flat car park, the fork and shock should offer similar support and shaft speeds under compression and rebound. Adjust the damping until they do.

A certain amount of trial and error will be involved in getting your setup correct. We recommend familiarising yourself with how the adjusters on your specific fork and/or shock work, and taking the time to experience how different setups perform on your favourite trails.

## What about suspension tuning?

If you want to dive even deeper into your suspension-tuning journey, you can look into tuning the internals of your fork and shock. Our Media / Luke Marshall

Modern mountain bike suspension is highly tunable right out of the box, offering adjustment to spring rates, air chamber volume and damping (via the external dials). However, if you want to go even deeper with your customisation, you can [tune the internals of your suspension](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/workshop/how-to-tune-mountain-bike-suspension). 

You can personalise the damping circuit of your suspension even further through custom pistons, shims and oil, controlling the oil flow and manipulating the suspension’s damping performance. This process is usually done through a professional tuning service. 

## What are the effects of different suspension adjustments?

Here's a summary of the effects of different suspension adjustments. Ian Linton / Our Media

Finding the correct suspension setup can be tricky, with different adjustments having knock-on effects on other variables. However, with time and patience, you will learn how your suspension works and how best to set it up for your riding and trail conditions. Use the following section to determine which adjustments will give you the desired results:

### Increasing spring rate (related to air pressure or coil-spring stiffness)

  * More support, less diving/squatting, higher ride height
  * Harder to bottom out
  * Harsher over bumps
  * Less sag, less traction, higher ride



### Adding volume spacers

  * Firmer end-stroke
  * Harder to bottom out
  * Harsher on medium-large hits where less travel is used
  * Only adds support towards the end of travel



### More low-speed compression

  * More stable and predictable feel because the bike is slower to dive or pitch as rider weight shifts
  * More to push against in corners and jump faces
  * More muted feel because more energy is dissipated in the damper rather than the spring
  * Suspension stays higher in its travel during rough sections
  * Reduced sensitivity, particularly over small-medium bumps
  * Less traction
  * More fatigue, particularly hand pain



### More high-speed compression

  * Less travel used during large impacts
  * Harder to bottom out
  * Suspension stays higher in its travel during rough sections
  * Harsher 'spikey' feel, particularly over medium-large impacts or landings



### More low-speed rebound damping

  * Calmer, more predictable feel
  * Rear suspension more settled on steep descents
  * Less chance of bucking on jumps or bouncing on landing
  * If set too slow, sensitivity and traction can be reduced
  * Suspension may 'pack down' during high-frequency bumps



### More high-speed rebound damping

  * Calmer and safer feel, particularly when using lots of travel, eg. jump take-offs and landings
  * Firmer HSR enables the LSR to be set faster (for better small-bump sensitivity) without getting bucked on jumps
  * Slower to recover, especially from deep in the stroke
  * Lighter HSR enables the LSR to be made firmer without the shock packing down. This will make the rebound slower in the beginning stroke, so can feel calmer on steep descents.



## What about coil-sprung suspension?

Coil suspension is becoming more and more niche. Kateland Clarke

To make things simpler, this guide focuses on air suspension because it’s far more common than [coil-sprung suspension](https://www.bikeradar.com/advice/buyers-guides/coil-vs-air) these days. If you have a coil fork or shock, most of the advice still applies.

The main difference is that instead of adjusting the spring rate by changing the air pressure, you’ll need to swap the coil spring.

Also, if your coil suspension is bottoming out too easily, you can’t add volume spacers to increase the end-stroke spring force like you would in an air spring.

Instead, either swap to a firmer spring rate (resulting in less sag) or increase high-speed compression damping, if possible.

Don't be tempted to use preload to make it firmer. Preloading the coil will result in less sag because it raises the ride height, but it will not affect the spring rate.

Preloading the shock also ruins the beginning-stroke sensitivity of the suspension and can cause it to top out. The preload collar should be just tight enough to stop the spring from rattling.

[Finlay AndersonContributor [](https://www.instagram.com/finlayanderson1) Finlay Anderson is a contributor at BikeRadar with more than five years of experience in the mountain bike industry. After getting hooked on enduro racing and moving to Scotland’s Tweed Valley, Finlay found his passion producing visual and written content for some of the bike industry's leading media outlets and brands. He has worked as an editor and photographer for Enduro MTB Magazine, written for MBR magazine and bagged the front cover of MBUK Issue 412. Finlay spent two years following the Enduro World Series, capturing exciting stories from the world's fastest racers. When he’s not working, Finlay loves sessioning turns at home and getting lost deep in the Scottish Borders on his gravel bike. As a keen rider, Finlay knows the importance of no-nonsense kit that stands up to the depths of Scottish winter.](https://www.bikeradar.com/author/finlay-anderson)

title: Mountain bike
text:
A mountain bike (MTB) or mountain bicycle is a bicycle designed for off-road cycling (mountain biking). Mountain bikes share some similarities with other bicycles, but incorporate features designed to enhance durability and performance in rough terrain, which often makes them heavier, more complex and less efficient on smooth surfaces. These typically include a suspension fork, large knobby tires, more durable wheels, more powerful brakes, straight, wide handlebars to improve balance and comfort over rough terrain, and wide-ratio gearing optimized for topography, application (e.g., steep climbing or fast descending) and a frame with a suspension mechanism for the rear wheel. Rear suspension is ubiquitous in heavier-duty bikes and now common even in lighter bikes. Dropper seat posts can be installed to allow the rider to quickly adjust the seat height (an elevated seat position is more effective for pedaling, but poses a hazard in aggressive maneuvers).
Mountain bikes are generally specialized for use on mountain trails, single track, fire roads, and other unpaved surfaces. In addition to being used to travel and recreate on those surfaces, many people use mountain bikes primarily on paved surfaces; some may prefer the upright position, plush ride, and stability that mountain bikes often have. Mountain biking terrain commonly has rocks, roots, loose dirt, and steep grades. Many trails have additional technical trail features (TTF) such as log piles, log rides, rock gardens, skinnies, gap jumps, and wall-rides. Mountain bikes are built to handle these types of terrain and features. The heavy-duty construction combined with stronger rims and wider tires has also made this style of bicycle popular with urban riders and couriers who must navigate through potholes and over curbs.
Since the start of the sport in the 1970s, many new subtypes of mountain biking have been developed, such as cross-country (XC), trail, all-mountain, enduro, freeride, downhill, and a variety of track and slalom types. Each of these place different demands on the bike, requiring different designs for optimal performance. MTB development has led to an increase in suspension travel, now often up to 8 inches (200 mm), and gearing up to 13 speed, to facilitate both climbing and rapid descents. Advances in gearing have also led to the ubiquity of "1x" drivetrains (pronounced "one-by"), simplifying the gearing to one chainring in the front and a wide range cassette at the rear, typically with 9 to 12 sprockets. 1x gearing reduces overall bike weight, increases ground clearance, and greatly simplifies the process of gear selection, but 2- or 3-ring drivetrains are still common on entry-level bikes.
The expressions "all terrain bicycle", "all terrain bike", and the acronym "ATB" are used as synonyms for "mountain bike", but some authors consider them passé.

title: Mountain bike
text:
The original mountain bikes were modified heavy cruiser bicycles used for freewheeling down mountain trails. The sport became popular in the 1970s in Northern California, US, with riders using older, single-speed balloon tire bicycles to ride down rugged hillsides. These modified bikes were called "ballooners" in California, "klunkers" in Colorado, and "dirt bombers" in Oregon. Joe Breeze, a bicycle frame builder, used this idea and developed what is considered the first mountain bike.
Road bicycle companies did not manufacture mountain bicycles using high-tech lightweight materials like M4 aluminum until the late 1970s and early 1980s. The first production mountain bike available was the 1979 Lawwill Pro Cruiser. The frame design was based on a frame that Don Koski fabricated from electrical conduit and a Schwinn Varsity frame. Mert Lawwill had Terry Knight of Oakland build the frames. The bikes sold for about $500 new and were made from 1979 through 1980 (approximate run of 600 bikes).
The first mass production mountain bike was the Specialized Stumpjumper, first produced in 1981. With the rising popularity of mountain bikes, Randolph (Randy) Ross, executive vice president of Ross Bicycles Inc., was quoted in the New York Times saying I'd say these bikes are one of the biggest things that ever happened to the biking industry. Its basic look constitutes "a total shift in image" for the industry.
Throughout the 1990s and 2000s, mountain biking moved from a little-known sport to a mainstream activity complete with an international racing circuit and a world championship, in addition to various free ride competitions, such as the FMB World Tour and the Red Bull Rampage.

title: Mountain bike
text:
Mountain bikes can usually be divided into four broad categories based on suspension configuration:

Rigid: A mountain bike with large, knobby tires and straight handlebars, but with neither front nor rear suspension.
Hardtail: A mountain bike equipped with a suspension fork for the front wheel, but otherwise a rigid frame.
Soft tail: A recent addition, a mountain bike with pivots in the frame but no rear shock. The flex of the frame absorbs some vibrations. These bikes are usually cross-country bikes.
Full suspension (or dual suspension): A mountain bike equipped with both front and rear suspension. The front suspension is usually a telescopic fork similar to that of a motorcycle, and the rear is suspended by a mechanical linkage with components for absorbing shock.

title: Mountain bike
text: Since the 1980s, mountain bikes have had anywhere from 7 to 36 speeds, with 1 to 4 chain-rings on the crankset and 5 to 12 sprockets in the cogset. 30-speed, 33-speed and 36-speed mountain bikes were originally found to be unworkable, as the mud-shedding capabilities of a 10-speed, 11-speed or 12-speed cassette, and the intricacies of a 10-speed, 11-speed or 12-speed rear derailleur were originally not found to be suitable combined with front shifters, although 10, 11 and 12 speed cassettes are now commonplace in single front chainring bicycles, and are also found on some mountain bikes. However, many pro-level mountain bikers have taken to using a narrower 10-speed road chain with a 9-speed setup in an effort to reduce the weight of their bike. In early 2009, component group SRAM announced their release of their XX groupset, which uses a 2-speed front derailleur, and a 10-speed rear derailleur and cassette, similar to that of a road bike. Mud-shedding capabilities of their 10-speed XX cassette are made suitable for MTB use by extensive machining of the cassette. Due to the time and cost involved in such a product, they were only aimed at top-end XC-racers. However, 10-speed has become the norm by 2011 and the market leader Shimano even offers its budget groupset "Alivio" in a 10-speed version. In July 2012, SRAM announced a 1×11 drivetrain called XX1 that does not make use of a front derailleur for lighter weight and simplicity. In the 2014 Commonwealth Games at Glasgow all leading riders used 1×11 drivetrains. SRAM's new 1×12 gearing was introduced in 2016 as SRAM Eagle. This gives a single chain ring bike better ability to climb.

title: Mountain bike
text: The critical angles in bicycle geometry are the head angle (the angle of the head tube), and the seat tube angle (the angle of the seat tube). These angles are measured from the horizontal, and drastically affect the rider position and performance characteristics of the bicycle. Mountain bike geometry will often feature a seat tube angle around 73 degrees, with a head tube angle of anywhere from 60 to 73 degrees. The intended application of the bike affects its geometry very heavily. In general, steeper angles (closer to 90 degrees from the horizontal) are more efficient for pedaling up hills and make for sharper handling. Slacker angles (leaning farther from the vertical) are preferred for high speeds and downhill stability.

title: Mountain bike
text:
In the past mountain bikes had a rigid frame and fork. In the early 1990s, the first mountain bikes with suspension forks were introduced. This made riding on rough terrain easier and less physically stressful. The first front suspension forks had about 11⁄2 to 2 inches (38 to 50 mm) of suspension travel. Once suspension was introduced, bikes with front suspension and rigid, non-suspended rear wheels, or "hardtails", became popular nearly overnight. While the hardtail design has the benefits of lower cost, less maintenance, and better pedaling efficiency, it is slowly losing popularity due to improvements in full suspension designs. Front fork suspensions are now available with 8 inches (200 mm) of travel or more (see above under Designs.)
Many new mountain bikes integrate a "full suspension" design known as dual suspension, meaning that both the front and rear wheel are fitted with a shock absorber in some form as the wheel attaches to the bike. This provides a smoother ride as the front and rear wheels can now travel up and down to absorb the force of obstacles striking the tires. Dual suspension bikes of a similar quality are considerably more expensive, heavier, and less efficient when pedaling, but are much faster on downhill and technical/rough sections than other mountain bikes. This is because, when a wheel strikes an obstacle, its tendency is to bounce up; this redirects some forward momentum, resulting in a loss of speed. Dual suspension bikes solve this problem by absorbing this upward force and transmitting it into the shocks of the front and rear wheels, drastically decreasing the translation of forward momentum into upward movement.

title: Mountain bike
text:
Most modern mountain bikes use disc brakes. They offer considerably improved stopping power (less lever pressure is required providing greater braking modulation) over rim brakes under all conditions  especially adverse conditions, because they are located at the center of the wheel (on the wheel hub).  They therefore remain drier and cleaner than wheel rims, which are more readily soiled or damaged. The disadvantage of disc brakes is their increased cost and often greater weight. Disc brakes do not allow heat to build up in the tires on long descents; instead, heat builds up in the rotor, which can become extremely hot.
There are two main types of mountain bike  disc brakes: mechanical disc brakes and hydraulic disc brakes. 
Mechanical disc brakes use a brake cable housed in a brake housing, which connects the brake lever to the brake caliper. When the lever is pulled, it tightens the cable, which in turn actuates the caliper and presses the brake pads against the rotor.
Hydraulic disc brakes, on the other hand, use a sealed hydraulic system instead of a cable. When the brake lever is pulled, it pressurizes the incompressible brake fluid (typically mineral oil or DOT fluid), which forces the brake pistons to move the pads against the rotor. Hydraulic systems generally provide stronger and more consistent braking performance compared to mechanical brakes.

title: Mountain bike
text:
Typical features of a mountain bike are very wide tires. The original 26-inch (660 mm) wheel diameter with approximately 2.125-inch (54.0 mm) width (ISO 559 mm rim diameter) is increasingly being displaced by 29-inch (740 mm) wheels with 2.35-inch (60 mm) width (ISO 622 mm rim diameter), as well as the 27.5-inch (700 mm) wheel diameter with 2.25-inch (57 mm) widths (ISO 584 mm rim diameter), particularly on smaller frame sizes for shorter riders. Mountain bikes with 24-inch (610 mm) wheels are also available, sometimes for dirt jumping, or as a junior bike.
Bicycle wheel sizes are not precise measurements: a 29-inch (740 mm) mountain bike wheel with a 24.5-inch (622 mm) bead seat diameter (the term, bead seat diameter (BSD), is used in the ETRTO tire and rim sizing system), and the average 29-inch (740 mm) mountain bike tire is (in ISO notation) 59-622 corresponds to an outside diameter of about 29.15 inches (740 mm).
24.5-inch (622 mm) wheels are standard on road bikes and are commonly known as 700C. In some countries, mainly in Continental Europe, 700C (622 mm) wheels are commonly called 28-inch wheels. 24-inch (610 mm) wheels are used for dirt jumping bikes and sometimes on freeride bikes, rear wheel only, as this makes the bike more maneuverable. 29-inch (740 mm) wheels were once used for only cross country purposes, but are now becoming more commonplace in other disciplines of mountain biking. A mountain bike with 29-inch wheels is often referred to as a 29er, and a bike with 27.5-inch wheels is called a 27.5 Mountain bike or as a marketing term ″650B bike".
Wheels come in a variety of widths, ranging from standard rims suitable for use with tires in the 1.90 to 2.10 in (48 to 53 mm) size, to 2.35 and 3.00 in (60 and 76 mm) widths popular with freeride and downhill bicycles. Although heavier wheelsets are favored in the freeride and downhill disciplines, advances in wheel technology continually shave weight off strong wheels. This is highly advantageous as rolling weight greatly affects handling and control, which are very important to the technical nature of freeride and downhill riding.

The widest wheel/tire widths, typically 3.8 inches (97 mm) or larger, are sometimes used by icebikers who use their mountain bikes for winter-time riding in snowy conditions.
Manufacturers produce bicycle tires with a wide variety of tread patterns to suit different needs. Among these styles are: slick street tires, street tires with a center ridge and outer tread, fully knobby, front-specific, rear-specific, and snow studded. Some tires can be specifically designed for use in certain weather (wet or dry) and terrain (hard, soft, muddy, etc.) conditions. Other tire designs attempt to be all-around applicable. Within the same intended application, more expensive tires tend to be lighter and have less rolling resistance. Sticky rubber tires are now available for use on freeride and downhill bikes. While these tires wear down more quickly, they provide greater traction in all conditions, especially during cornering. Tires and rims are available in either tubed or tubeless designs, with tubeless tires recently (2004) gaining favor for their pinch flat resistance.
Tires also come with tubes, tubeless and tubeless-ready. Tires with tubes are the standard design and the easiest to use and maintain. Tubeless tires are significantly lighter and often have better performance because you can run them at a lower tire pressure which results in better traction and increasing rolling resistance. Tubeless-ready tires are tires that can use tubes or go tubeless. A liquid sealant is used without the tube to secure the seal to the rim. Popular tire manufacturers include Wilderness Trail Bikes, Schwalbe, Maxxis, Nokian, Michelin, Continental, Tioga, Kenda, Hutchinson, Specialized and Panaracer.

title: Mountain bike
text: Mountain bikes are available in tandem configurations. For example, Cannondale and Santana Cycles offer ones without suspension, while Ellsworth, Nicolai, and Ventana manufacture tandems with full suspension.

title: Mountain bike
text: MTB that has specialised equipment or is modified to make it accessible to individuals with varying mobility requirements. It can be two-wheeled, three-wheeled or four-wheeled. It can also have electrical motor assistance.

title: Mountain bike
text:
Vintage MTB Day is an event or gathering where enthusiasts celebrate and ride classic, older mountain bikes (MTBs). The occasion typically features bicycles from the 1970s, 1980s, and early 1990s, showcasing the evolution of mountain bike technology and design. The event was established to honor and recognize the pioneers of mountain biking.
Held every 13 August in cities and countries around the world, Vintage MTB Day is open to participants of all backgrounds and encourages community involvement through group rides, exhibitions, and discussions of vintage bicycle culture.

title: Mountain bike
text:
Across different countries, Vintage MTB Day is marked by events focused on classic mountain bikes. Common activities include:

Fun Ride & Retro Gathering – group rides where participants use vintage MTBs and wear retro cycling gear.
Bike Exhibitions – collectors display frames, components, and accessories such as cantilever brakes, thumb shifters, and semi-slick tires from the 1980s.
Workshops & Talks – sessions on restoring old bikes, mountain biking history, and maintaining classic components.
Social Media Campaigns – sharing photos or videos of vintage MTBs with the hashtag #VintageMTBDay.

title: Mountain bike
text: Beyond nostalgia, the celebration promotes sustainability by encouraging the restoration and continued use of older bicycles. Vintage MTB Day inspires a new generation of cyclists to learn about mountain biking history, reduce waste through refurbishing, and preserve cycling culture across generations.

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
Sources:

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
Mud, dirt, and trail debris wear down components like chains, brakes, and suspension, causing expensive repairs if neglected. Well-maintained bikes deliver reliable handling and extended component lifespan. Your MTB always signals when it needs attention — squeaks, slips, and wobbles are its language.

---

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
### 1. Rinse the Bike (3 min)

Use a garden hose with moderate pressure — avoid pressure washers, which force water past seals. Rinse top to bottom:
- Frame and fork
- Shock and wheels
- Drivetrain
- Underside of downtube
- Linkage areas (full-suspension bikes)
- Bottom bracket zone

For caked mud, let water soak briefly before rinsing. A soft brush helps dislodge stubborn debris.

### 2. Chain Clean and Lube (4 min)

A dirty chain shifts poorly, wears faster, and damages cassettes and chainrings.

**Quick clean:** Run a clean rag around the chain while backpedalling.

**Deeper clean:** Use a chain cleaning device with degreaser every few rides.

**Lubrication:** Apply one drop of lube per link while slowly backpedalling. Backpedal a dozen times to work it in, then wipe excess with a clean rag.

**Lube selection lifehack:**
- Wet lube for wet/muddy conditions — stays longer but attracts dirt
- Dry lube for dry conditions — sheds dirt better but washes off easily
- Less is more: too much lube just attracts more grit

### 3. Brake Pad Inspection (2 min)

Look through the top of each brake caliper. Check friction material thickness — most pads have a wear indicator groove.
- Replace when worn to 1–1.5 mm remaining
- Check rotor scoring by running a finger lightly along the braking surface when cool
- Spongy lever or lever reaching the bar = worn pads or air in the hydraulic system

### 4. Tyre Inspection (2 min)

Run hands around tread and sidewall:
- **Embedded debris:** Remove thorns, flints, or wire fragments immediately
- **Cuts and gashes:** Deep cuts exposing the casing need replacement soon
- **Sidewall condition:** Bulging sidewalls = compromised integrity
- **Knob wear:** Excessively worn knobs reduce grip in mud and corners
- Check pressure with a gauge — tubeless tyres lose 1–2 PSI between rides, especially in cold weather

### 5. Bolt Check (2 min)

Quick squeeze and wiggle — not a full re-torque, just confirming nothing loosened:
- Handlebar and stem bolts
- Wheel axles (lift and rock side-to-side)
- Saddle and seatpost / dropper collar
- Brake caliper mounts
- Crank bolts (rock each arm laterally)

**Lifehack:** Use a torque wrench for stem bolts and carbon components — over-tightening damages aluminum and carbon.

### 6. Suspension Wipe (2 min)

Wipe exposed fork stanchion tubes with a clean, soft cloth. Remove dirt and grit that would otherwise drag past seals into fork internals. Do the same for the rear shock shaft. Weeping oil or drips = seal failure needing attention.

---

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
- **Drivetrain assessment:** Check for shark-fin-shaped teeth on chainring or cassette (sign of wear). Use a chain wear tool — replace chain at 0.5–0.75% wear and your cassette/chainring will last 2–3x longer.
- **Wheel check:** Spin each wheel and watch rim alignment relative to brake pads. Squeeze pairs of spokes — all should feel similar in tension.
- **Suspension air pressure:** Fork pressure deflates naturally over time — check bi-weekly.
- **Pivot bearings (full suspension):** Rock rear wheel side-to-side while holding frame. Any knocking or play beyond the hub = worn pivot bearings.
- **Inspect brake pads:** Replace if thinner than 1–2 mm.
- **Torque bolts:** Go through all fasteners with a torque wrench.

---

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
| Frequency | Tasks |
|---|---|
| After every ride | Rinse bike, wipe chain, apply light lube, check tire pressure, tighten axles, wipe suspension stanchions |
| Every 3–5 rides | Inspect brake pads, check suspension air pressure, measure chain wear, torque bolts, inspect tires |
| Every 50 hours | Lower leg fork service: clean/re-grease bushings, replace dust seals and foam rings, refill oil |
| Every 3–6 months | Service fork lowers, bleed brakes if needed, deep-clean drivetrain, refresh tubeless sealant, inspect frame |
| Every 100–150 hours | Full suspension service: fork damper, rear shock, brake bleed, headset, bottom bracket, hubs, drivetrain |
| Every 6–12 months | Service rear shock, full frame inspection (bearings, headset, bottom bracket) |
| Annually | Full overhaul: pivot bearing replacement, cable/hose replacement, frame inspection, wheel rebuild assessment |

---

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
- **Track your ride hours** using a GPS unit or app (Strava, TrailForks) to schedule service based on actual use, not just calendar time.
- **Avoid high-pressure washers** — they force water past seals and into bearings.
- **Store your bike indoors** or in a sheltered area. Use hooks for elevation. UV, rain, and humidity cause rust and deterioration.
- **Tubeless sealant dries out** — top it up every 2–3 months or after a puncture repair.
- **Less chain lube is more** — the goal is lubrication inside the chain links, not a shiny coat outside.
- **Use manufacturer-recommended products** for suspension lubrication — wrong products degrade seals.
- **Lockout compression** should only be used on roads or smooth singletrack — active suspension helps traction on climbs too.
- **Build the 15-minute habit** consistently — small regular effort dramatically extends component life and reduces costly repairs.
- **When to go to a shop:** Persistent gear skipping after cleaning, suspension leaks, wobbly wheels or bent rotors that resist DIY fixes.

---

title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks
text:
| Component | Replace When |
|---|---|
| Chain | 0.5–0.75% wear (use chain wear tool) |
| Brake pads | Less than 1–2 mm of friction material remaining |
| Cassette / chainring | After 2–3 chains, or when teeth are shark-fin shaped |
| Fork lower leg oil | Every 50 hours |
| Rear shock full service | Every 100–150 hours or if wheezing/clunking |
| Tubeless sealant | Every 2–3 months |
| Hydraulic brake fluid | When fluid darkens |
| Carbon components | Every 3–6 years |

title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
text: Source: https://www.liv-cycling.com/global/campaigns/how-to-set-up-mountain-bike-suspension/20654

title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
text:
Riding a full-suspension mountain bike instead of a hardtail generally allows you to traverse technical terrain with less effort and maintain better traction on rough or loose surfaces—but only with proper suspension setup. Poor suspension configuration is a primary reason many riders experience suboptimal performance on their bikes.

### Suspension Terms

**Travel**: The amount of suspension compression available, measured in millimeters. For example, the Liv Hail offers 160mm while the Pique provides 120mm.

**Fork**: Front suspension on a mountain bike.

**Shock**: Rear suspension on a mountain bike.

**Stanchion**: The polished component of the fork or shock that extends into the fork lowers or shock body.

**Sag**: The travel consumed simply by sitting on the bike. A sag test measures adequate air pressure in your fork or shock. Ideally, you should compress 25-35% of total travel when seated.

**Shock Pump**: The specialized device for pressurizing suspension components. Standard bike tire pumps are incompatible.

**Compression Damping**: A control knob on some forks and shocks regulating oil flow through internal ports. Greater compression damping produces a firmer feel.

**Rebound Damping**: This adjustment controls how quickly your fork or shock returns to its resting position. Increased rebound damping slows the return speed.

title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
text:
1. Recruit a friend to stabilize your front wheel during testing.

2. If your suspension includes compression damping knobs, position them fully open.

3. Stand on the pedals and bounce several times while applying slight front-end pressure, mimicking riding conditions. This mobilizes the internal oil and prevents stiction.

4. While maintaining your typical riding position, have your assistant reset the rubber o-rings on your stanchion.

5. Gently lower yourself onto the saddle, then dismount.

6. Examine your stanchion. The o-ring should sit approximately one-quarter down from the top. If it's positioned less than one-quarter down, you have excessive air pressure—use your shock pump to release air. If positioned more than one-quarter down, you lack sufficient pressure—add air accordingly.

metadata.section: How to Set Sag on a Mountain Bike

title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
text:
After confirming correct sag, proceed to rebound adjustment. Rebound tuning remains highly personal, yet improper settings significantly compromise riding quality. As a starting point, determine your total available clicks by rotating the rebound knob fully counterclockwise (toward the jackalope symbol on RockShox or minus sign on Fox) to eliminate all damping. Count clicks while rotating clockwise (toward the turtle on RockShox or plus sign on Fox). Return halfway through your count.

Test the rebound feel by compressing and releasing either the saddle (for shock rebound) or handlebars (for fork rebound) using open-palm pressure without gripping. Your suspension should return promptly to center without bouncing excessively. Adjust clicks as needed. "Slower" rebound requires additional damping (turtle/plus), while "faster" rebound needs less damping (jackalope/minus).

Fine-tuning happens best during actual riding. If your bike feels like it's launching you on landings or the ride feels harsh over technical features, reduce rebound speed. If the suspension "packs up" over consecutive bumps and fails returning to neutral, increase rebound speed.

title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
text:
Every fork and shock differs in damping options—some offer high and low-speed compression settings, others provide two or three options, and some have none. Review your specific suspension manufacturer's documentation and recommendations.

One key consideration: if your suspension provides a lockout compression position, use it exclusively on roads, gravel, or smooth singletrack. With correctly configured sag, maintain active suspension while climbing technical terrain. Suspension assists traction when ascending rocks and roots just as it does descending—keeping tires grounded improves traction throughout.

Suspension setup involves considerable technical depth. The primary advice: experiment with your fork and shock adjustments. Learn what each control does and discover your preferred settings through hands-on testing.
